question: A physician describes a surgical specialty treating ear, nose, and throat disorders. Which single-word name best identifies this specialty?
cited_learning_objective: Define the name and scope of the specialty.

options.text: Otolaryngology
options.rationale: Correct because the lecture states the specialty combining ear, nose, and throat is called Otolaryngology; this is the single-word name used in the slides.

options.text: Rhinolaryngology
options.rationale: Wrong because the lecture explains the historical roots include rhino and laryn but the accepted single-word specialty name is Otolaryngology; it would be correct only if the slides named Rhinolaryngology as the final term.

options.text: Ophthalmology
options.rationale: Wrong because ophthalmology treats the eye; the lecture notes ophthalmology was historically combined but is a separate specialty; it would be correct only if the focus were ocular disease.

options.text: Otorhinolaryngology
options.rationale: Wrong because although Otorhinolaryngology is a historical full form, the lecture indicates the specialty is commonly shortened to Otolaryngology; it would be correct only if the full historical name were requested.

options.text: Headneck Surgery
options.rationale: Wrong because the lecture uses Otolaryngology or ENT as the formal specialty name; 'Headneck Surgery' is not the single-word specialty term used in the slides.

question: Which subspecialty focuses primarily on cochlear implants, cholesteatoma surgery, and ossicular reconstruction?
cited_learning_objective: Identify the scope of Otology/Neuro-otology within ENT.

options.text: Otology Neuro-otology specialty
options.rationale: Correct because the lecture lists cochlear implants, cholesteatomas, and ossicular reconstruction under Otology/Neuro-otology.

options.text: Rhinology sinonasal specialty
options.rationale: Wrong because Rhinology addresses sinonasal disease and skull base tumors per the lecture; it would be correct only if the procedures listed were sinonasal in nature.

options.text: Laryngology airway and voice specialty
options.rationale: Wrong because Laryngology focuses on voice, swallowing, and airway disorders; it would be correct only if cochlear implants or ossicular work were part of laryngeal practice.

options.text: Facial plastics reconstruction specialty
options.rationale: Wrong because Facial Plastics handles cosmetic and reconstructive facial procedures; it would be correct only if ossicular reconstruction were considered facial reconstruction in the lecture.

options.text: Head and neck oncology specialty
options.rationale: Wrong because Head and Neck Oncology treats cancers and free flap reconstruction; it would be correct only if cholesteatoma and cochlear implants were oncologic procedures.

question: A child with recurrent throat infections meets frequency thresholds for surgery. Which indication from the lecture supports tonsil removal?
cited_learning_objective: Recall pediatric tonsillectomy indications based on recurrent infection counts.

options.text: Seven infections in the last year
options.rationale: Correct because the lecture lists '7 in last year' as an indication for tonsillectomy for recurrent tonsillitis.

options.text: Two infections in the last year
options.rationale: Wrong because the lecture specifies higher frequency thresholds (e.g., 7 in one year); it would be correct only if the guideline threshold were lower in the slides.

options.text: Five infections ever in lifetime
options.rationale: Wrong because the lecture requires specific annualized counts (e.g., 5 per year over two years), not a lifetime total; it would be correct only if the criterion were lifetime frequency.

options.text: Three infections in the last five years
options.rationale: Wrong because the lecture specifies 3 per year in the last three years, not three over five years; it would be correct only if the timeframe matched the guideline.

options.text: One severe infection requiring admission
options.rationale: Wrong because the lecture emphasizes recurrent counts and sleep disordered breathing as indications; it would be correct only if the slides listed single severe admissions as an indication.

question: Which postoperative counseling point is emphasized for pediatric tonsillectomy in the lecture?
cited_learning_objective: Describe postoperative considerations and risks after pediatric tonsillectomy.

options.text: Avoid narcotics use after surgery
options.rationale: Correct because the lecture explicitly advises avoidance of narcotics and recommends Tylenol/Motrin for pain control.

options.text: Resume contact sports immediately after surgery
options.rationale: Wrong because the lecture does not recommend immediate return to sports; it would be correct only if the slides stated no activity restrictions.

options.text: Expect no bleeding risk after two weeks
options.rationale: Wrong because the lecture highlights bleeding risk at 12-14 days; it would be correct only if the lecture stated bleeding risk resolves earlier.

options.text: Hospitalize all children for three days
options.rationale: Wrong because the lecture notes tonsillectomy is outpatient except for children under three or syndromic patients; it would be correct only if the slides mandated routine admission.

options.text: Use routine postoperative antibiotics always
options.rationale: Wrong because the lecture does not mandate routine antibiotics post-tonsillectomy; it would be correct only if the slides recommended antibiotics universally.

question: Which finding most suggests conductive hearing loss rather than sensorineural loss based on the lecture categories?
cited_learning_objective: Differentiate conductive versus sensorineural hearing loss categories.

options.text: Middle ear fluid causing sound conduction impairment
options.rationale: Correct because the lecture lists middle ear fluid as a cause of conductive hearing loss affecting sound conduction.

options.text: Bilateral symmetric high-frequency loss on audiogram
options.rationale: Wrong because symmetric high-frequency loss is characteristic of sensorineural loss; it would be correct only if the lecture associated that pattern with conductive loss.

options.text: Sudden unilateral profound loss without fluid
options.rationale: Wrong because sudden sensorineural hearing loss is described separately in the lecture; it would be correct only if sudden loss were conductive in the slides.

options.text: Otosclerosis causing inner ear hair cell damage
options.rationale: Wrong because the lecture lists otosclerosis as a conductive pathology (stapes fixation), not inner hair cell damage; it would be correct only if otosclerosis were described as sensorineural.

options.text: Cerumen impaction with normal tympanic membrane mobility
options.rationale: Wrong because cerumen impaction causes conductive loss but would usually affect tympanic membrane mobility; it would be correct only if mobility remained normal per the lecture.

question: A patient presents with sudden unilateral hearing decline. According to the lecture, which immediate treatment option is appropriate?
cited_learning_objective: Apply lecture guidance for management of sudden sensorineural hearing loss.

options.text: Oral corticosteroids as initial therapy
options.rationale: Correct because the lecture lists oral steroids (or intratympanic steroids) as treatment options for sudden sensorineural hearing loss.

options.text: Immediate tympanoplasty to repair membrane
options.rationale: Wrong because tympanoplasty addresses tympanic membrane perforation or chronic middle ear disease, not sudden sensorineural loss; it would be correct only if the hearing loss were conductive with a perforation.

options.text: Observation without intervention for six months
options.rationale: Wrong because the lecture recommends steroid therapy for SSNHL rather than prolonged observation; it would be correct only if the slides advised conservative watchful waiting.

options.text: Start topical nasal steroids only
options.rationale: Wrong because nasal steroids treat sinonasal disease and middle ear eustachian dysfunction, not primary sudden sensorineural hearing loss; it would be correct only if the hearing loss were due to nasal pathology per the lecture.

options.text: Immediate cochlear implantation in emergency
options.rationale: Wrong because cochlear implants are for severe sensorineural loss after evaluation, not immediate emergency treatment; it would be correct only if the lecture recommended immediate implantation.

question: Which diagnostic maneuver is indicated to evaluate brief positional spinning episodes per the lecture?
cited_learning_objective: Recall the bedside test used for positional vertigo evaluation.

options.text: Dix-Hallpike positional testing maneuver
options.rationale: Correct because the lecture names the Dix-Hallpike maneuver as the test for positional vertigo such as BPPV.

options.text: Romberg static balance testing
options.rationale: Wrong because Romberg assesses proprioception and balance, not specifically positional vertigo; it would be correct only if the lecture equated Romberg with positional testing.

options.text: Head impulse rapid rotation test
options.rationale: Wrong because the lecture emphasizes Dix-Hallpike for BPPV; head impulse tests vestibulo-ocular reflex but is not the positional test named in the slides.

options.text: Caloric irrigation of external ear canal
options.rationale: Wrong because caloric testing assesses vestibular function but is not the bedside Dix-Hallpike positional maneuver; it would be correct only if the lecture recommended calorics for brief positional episodes.

options.text: Tuning fork Weber and Rinne tests
options.rationale: Wrong because Weber and Rinne evaluate conductive versus sensorineural hearing loss, not positional vertigo; it would be correct only if the complaint were hearing rather than vertigo.

question: Which peripheral vestibular diagnosis is characterized by brief episodes triggered by head turning, as described in the lecture?
cited_learning_objective: Identify clinical features of common peripheral vestibular disorders.

options.text: Benign paroxysmal positional vertigo posterior canal
options.rationale: Correct because the lecture states BPPV causes brief episodes usually aggravated by turning and commonly involves the posterior semicircular canal.

options.text: Vestibular neuritis with three day vertigo
options.rationale: Wrong because vestibular neuritis causes prolonged intense vertigo over days, not brief turning-triggered episodes; it would be correct only if the episodes lasted days.

options.text: Meniere disease with fluctuating hearing loss
options.rationale: Wrong because Meniere’s presents with aural fullness, fluctuating SNHL, and episodic vertigo, not brief positional episodes; it would be correct only if hearing fluctuation were the dominant feature.

options.text: Orthostatic hypotension causing lightheadedness
options.rationale: Wrong because orthostatic hypotension causes lightheadedness on standing rather than brief positional spinning; it would be correct only if syncope-like symptoms were described.

options.text: Central vestibular stroke with focal deficits
options.rationale: Wrong because central causes often have neurological signs and are not the classic brief positional BPPV described in the lecture; it would be correct only if focal neuro deficits were present.

question: A patient with chronic nasal obstruction and polyps also has asthma and aspirin sensitivity. Which triad name from the lecture best describes this combination?
cited_learning_objective: Recognize Samter's triad components in rhinologic disease.

options.text: Samter’s triad of polyps asthma aspirin
options.rationale: Correct because the lecture explicitly names Samter’s triad as polyps, asthma, and aspirin sensitivity.

options.text: Kartagener syndrome of sinus situs inversus
options.rationale: Wrong because Kartagener syndrome involves situs inversus and bronchiectasis, not the polyps-asthma-aspirin combination; it would be correct only if situs inversus were present.

options.text: Cystic fibrosis with nasal polyps and infection
options.rationale: Wrong because while cystic fibrosis can cause polyps, the lecture specifically identifies Samter’s triad for polyps with asthma and aspirin sensitivity; it would be correct only if CF were documented.

options.text: Allergic fungal sinusitis with eosinophilia and polyps
options.rationale: Wrong because allergic fungal sinusitis is a distinct entity; it would be correct only if fungal allergic disease were the primary cause per the lecture.

options.text: Nonallergic rhinitis with vasomotor symptoms
options.rationale: Wrong because nonallergic rhinitis lacks the asthma and aspirin sensitivity components of Samter’s triad; it would be correct only if asthma and aspirin sensitivity were absent.

question: Which management option is listed in the lecture for chronic rhinosinusitis with nasal polyps when medical therapy fails?
cited_learning_objective: List surgical options for chronic rhinosinusitis management.

options.text: Functional endoscopic sinus surgery in operating room
options.rationale: Correct because the lecture lists FESS (endoscopic sinus surgery) as a surgical option for CRS when medical therapy is insufficient.

options.text: Long-term systemic antibiotics without surgery
options.rationale: Wrong because while antibiotics may be used, the lecture includes surgery (FESS) as an option when medical therapy fails; it would be correct only if surgery were contraindicated.

options.text: Balloon sinuplasty exclusively in all patients
options.rationale: Wrong because the lecture lists balloon dilation as an option but distinguishes office versus OR and does not state it is exclusive for all patients; it would be correct only if the slides recommended balloon for every case.

options.text: Immediate paranasal sinus open external approach
options.rationale: Wrong because the lecture emphasizes endoscopic approaches (FESS) and balloon techniques rather than routine open external approaches; it would be correct only if external surgery were specified.

options.text: Allergy shots as sole definitive therapy
options.rationale: Wrong because allergy immunotherapy is part of management but the lecture lists surgery as an option when medical therapy including allergy treatment fails; it would be correct only if immunotherapy cured all cases.

question: A patient reports chronic purulent nasal drainage for three months with facial pressure. According to the lecture definitions, how is this duration classified?
cited_learning_objective: Recall time-based definitions for acute versus chronic sinusitis.

options.text: Chronic sinusitis lasting more than twelve weeks
options.rationale: Correct because the lecture defines chronic sinusitis as symptoms persisting for more than 12 weeks.

options.text: Acute sinusitis lasting less than four weeks
options.rationale: Wrong because acute sinusitis is defined as under four weeks per the lecture; it would be correct only if the duration were shorter.

options.text: Acute recurrent sinusitis with continuous symptoms
options.rationale: Wrong because acute recurrent sinusitis involves multiple discrete episodes without persistent symptoms between them, not continuous three-month symptoms; it would be correct only if episodes were intermittent.

options.text: Subacute sinusitis lasting four to twelve weeks
options.rationale: Wrong because the lecture does not define subacute explicitly; it would be correct only if the lecture used that intermediate category for this duration.

options.text: Viral rhinitis unrelated to sinusitis diagnosis
options.rationale: Wrong because purulent drainage and facial pressure for three months meet chronic sinusitis criteria in the lecture; it would be correct only if symptoms were brief and viral.

question: Which test does the lecture mention for evaluating allergic contributors to chronic rhinosinusitis?
cited_learning_objective: Identify allergy testing modalities referenced in rhinology management.

options.text: Skin testing or RAST blood allergy testing
options.rationale: Correct because the lecture lists skin testing and RAST (radioallergosorbent test) as allergy evaluation options.

options.text: Complete blood count with differential only
options.rationale: Wrong because CBC is not listed as the primary allergy test in the lecture; it would be correct only if the slides recommended CBC for allergy diagnosis.

options.text: Nasal endoscopy without allergy testing
options.rationale: Wrong because nasal endoscopy assesses anatomy and polyps but the lecture specifically mentions skin and RAST testing for allergies; it would be correct only if allergy testing were omitted.

options.text: CT scan of sinuses as allergy test
options.rationale: Wrong because CT evaluates sinus anatomy and disease extent, not allergic sensitization; it would be correct only if CT findings were used to infer allergy per the lecture.

options.text: Pulmonary function testing for sinonasal allergy
options.rationale: Wrong because PFTs assess lower airway function and are not listed as primary allergy tests in the lecture; it would be correct only if lower airway testing were equated with sinonasal allergy.

question: A patient with unilateral middle ear effusion and no other symptoms should prompt evaluation for which concerning diagnosis per the lecture?
cited_learning_objective: Apply lecture guidance to identify red flags for unilateral middle ear effusion.

options.text: Nasopharyngeal tumor evaluation recommended
options.rationale: Correct because the lecture warns that unilateral middle ear effusion may indicate a nasopharyngeal tumor and warrants evaluation.

options.text: Routine bilateral ear tube placement without imaging
options.rationale: Wrong because the lecture suggests unilateral MEE raises concern for nasopharyngeal tumor rather than immediate routine bilateral tube placement; it would be correct only if effusion were bilateral and recurrent.

options.text: Immediate cochlear implant referral for hearing loss
options.rationale: Wrong because cochlear implants address sensorineural loss, not unilateral middle ear effusion; it would be correct only if sensorineural loss were documented.

options.text: Observation only for six months without workup
options.rationale: Wrong because the lecture indicates unilateral MEE should prompt evaluation for nasopharyngeal tumor rather than prolonged observation; it would be correct only if bilateral and transient.

options.text: Start intranasal steroids as sole action
options.rationale: Wrong because while nasal steroids may help eustachian dysfunction, the lecture specifically flags unilateral MEE as concerning for tumor and needing evaluation; it would be correct only if tumor risk were excluded.

question: Which of the following is the most common peripheral cause of vertigo listed in the lecture?
cited_learning_objective: Recall the most common peripheral vestibular diagnosis.

options.text: Benign paroxysmal positional vertigo posterior
options.rationale: Correct because the lecture states BPPV is the most common peripheral cause of vertigo, often in the posterior semicircular canal.

options.text: Vestibular neuritis with prolonged vertigo
options.rationale: Wrong because vestibular neuritis is common but the lecture identifies BPPV as the most common peripheral cause; it would be correct only if neuritis were more prevalent per the slides.

options.text: Meniere disease with fluctuating hearing
options.rationale: Wrong because Meniere’s is less common and is a diagnosis of exclusion per the lecture; it would be correct only if prevalence data differed in the lecture.

options.text: Central vestibular migraine variant
options.rationale: Wrong because central causes like vestibular migraine are not classified as the most common peripheral cause; it would be correct only if central causes were more prevalent.

options.text: Orthostatic hypotension causing dizziness
options.rationale: Wrong because orthostatic hypotension causes lightheadedness rather than peripheral vertigo; it would be correct only if the symptom described were presyncope.

question: Which bedside therapeutic maneuver is taught in the lecture to treat posterior canal positional vertigo?
cited_learning_objective: Identify the Epley maneuver as a treatment for posterior canal BPPV.

options.text: Epley canalith repositioning maneuver
options.rationale: Correct because the lecture presents the Epley maneuver as the treatment for posterior semicircular canal BPPV.

options.text: Valsalva maneuver for middle ear pressure equalization
options.rationale: Wrong because Valsalva addresses middle ear pressure, not canalith repositioning for BPPV; it would be correct only if the problem were eustachian tube dysfunction.

options.text: Semont liberatory maneuver for central vertigo
options.rationale: Wrong because Semont is another repositioning technique but the lecture specifically highlights Epley for posterior canal BPPV; it would be correct only if Semont were the maneuver emphasized.

options.text: Brandt-Daroff exercises for chronic migraine vertigo
options.rationale: Wrong because Brandt-Daroff exercises are habituation exercises and the lecture emphasizes Epley for acute posterior canal BPPV; it would be correct only if habituation were the recommended initial therapy.

options.text: Caloric stimulation to reposition canaliths
options.rationale: Wrong because caloric testing assesses vestibular function and does not reposition canaliths; it would be correct only if the lecture described calorics as therapeutic.

question: Which symptom cluster in the lecture best characterizes Meniere-like disease?
cited_learning_objective: Recognize the clinical triad associated with Meniere’s disease.

options.text: Aural fullness episodic vertigo fluctuating SNHL
options.rationale: Correct because the lecture describes Meniere’s as aural fullness, spinning vertigo, nausea/vomiting, and fluctuating sensorineural hearing loss.

options.text: Continuous imbalance without hearing changes
options.rationale: Wrong because Meniere’s includes fluctuating hearing loss and episodic vertigo, not continuous imbalance without hearing change; it would be correct only if hearing remained stable.

options.text: Brief positional spinning without aural fullness
options.rationale: Wrong because brief positional spinning suggests BPPV rather than Meniere’s; it would be correct only if positional triggers and lack of fullness were present.

options.text: Three days of intense vertigo with normal hearing
options.rationale: Wrong because three days of intense vertigo with normal hearing aligns with vestibular neuritis per the lecture, not Meniere’s; it would be correct only if hearing were fluctuating.

options.text: Lightheadedness on standing with syncope episodes
options.rationale: Wrong because orthostatic symptoms differ from Meniere’s vestibular and auditory symptoms; it would be correct only if presyncope were the main complaint.

question: Which management step does the lecture list for a tympanic membrane perforation when considering repair?
cited_learning_objective: Describe management options for tympanic membrane perforation.

options.text: Observation versus surgical repair considered
options.rationale: Correct because the lecture lists observation versus repair as management options for tympanic membrane perforation.

options.text: Immediate cochlear implantation recommended
options.rationale: Wrong because cochlear implantation is not a treatment for tympanic membrane perforation; it would be correct only if the perforation caused irreversible profound SNHL per the lecture.

options.text: Routine systemic steroids for membrane healing
options.rationale: Wrong because the lecture does not recommend routine systemic steroids for tympanic membrane perforation; it would be correct only if steroids were specified for this indication.

options.text: Myringotomy with tube placement always required
options.rationale: Wrong because the lecture lists observation or repair, not mandatory tube placement; it would be correct only if persistent middle ear effusion or other indications existed.

options.text: Immediate radical mastoidectomy for all perforations
options.rationale: Wrong because radical mastoidectomy is not the standard for simple tympanic membrane perforation per the lecture; it would be correct only if extensive cholesteatoma or mastoid disease were present.

question: Which ENT subspecialty listed in the lecture primarily manages swallowing disorders and vocal cord paralysis?
cited_learning_objective: Identify the scope of Laryngology within ENT.

options.text: Laryngology voice and swallowing specialty
options.rationale: Correct because the lecture lists Laryngology as handling difficult airways, vocal cord tumors, swallowing disorders, and vocal cord paralysis.

options.text: Otology hearing and balance specialty
options.rationale: Wrong because Otology focuses on hearing and balance rather than voice and swallowing; it would be correct only if the question asked about auditory disorders.

options.text: Rhinology sinonasal and skull base specialty
options.rationale: Wrong because Rhinology treats sinonasal disease and skull base tumors, not primary voice and swallowing disorders; it would be correct only if sinonasal pathology were the focus.

options.text: Facial plastics cosmetic reconstruction specialty
options.rationale: Wrong because Facial Plastics addresses cosmetic and reconstructive facial procedures, not vocal cord or swallowing disorders; it would be correct only if facial reconstruction were required.

options.text: Pediatric ENT tonsils and tubes specialty
options.rationale: Wrong because Pediatrics focuses on tonsils, adenoids, and ear tubes in children, not adult swallowing and vocal cord paralysis; it would be correct only if the patient were a child with those issues.

question: A 50-year-old patient develops sudden unilateral hearing loss without trauma. According to the lecture, which diagnostic distinction is most important to determine initial management?
cited_learning_objective: Differentiate sudden sensorineural from conductive causes to guide immediate therapy.

options.text: Sensorineural versus conductive hearing loss distinction
options.rationale: Correct because the lecture emphasizes distinguishing conductive from sensorineural loss; SSNHL is treated with steroids whereas conductive causes have different management.

options.text: Bilateral versus unilateral symmetry assessment only
options.rationale: Wrong because while symmetry matters, the lecture highlights the conductive versus sensorineural distinction as key for management; it would be correct only if symmetry alone dictated therapy.

options.text: Age of onset over versus under fifty years
options.rationale: Wrong because age alone is not the primary determinant of immediate management per the lecture; it would be correct only if age thresholds were specified for treatment.

options.text: Presence of ear wax as sole diagnostic criterion
options.rationale: Wrong because cerumen can cause conductive loss but the lecture stresses broader differentiation; it would be correct only if wax were the only abnormal finding.

options.text: History of chronic sinusitis as main factor
options.rationale: Wrong because chronic sinusitis is not the primary determinant for sudden unilateral hearing loss management per the lecture; it would be correct only if sinus disease directly caused the hearing change.

question: Which feature in the lecture differentiates dizziness from vertigo in clinical description?
cited_learning_objective: Distinguish vertigo from other forms of dizziness using symptom descriptors.

options.text: Vertigo is the illusion of motion spinning
options.rationale: Correct because the lecture defines vertigo specifically as the illusion of motion, often described as spinning.

options.text: Lightheadedness always indicates inner ear disease
options.rationale: Wrong because the lecture states lightheadedness is a nonspecific symptom (feeling woozy) and not always ear related; it would be correct only if lightheadedness were defined as vestibular.

options.text: Syncope and vertigo are synonymous terms
options.rationale: Wrong because the lecture differentiates syncope (passing out) from vertigo; they are not synonymous and would be correct only if the lecture conflated them.

options.text: Dizziness exclusively means vestibular dysfunction
options.rationale: Wrong because the lecture emphasizes dizziness is non-specific and can reflect many causes beyond vestibular dysfunction; it would be correct only if dizziness were narrowly defined.

options.text: Lightheadedness always requires ENT referral
options.rationale: Wrong because the lecture indicates lightheadedness may have many causes and is not always ENT-related; it would be correct only if ENT evaluation were universally required.

question: Which management option for chronic rhinosinusitis mentioned in the lecture can be performed in the office setting?
cited_learning_objective: Differentiate office-based versus operating room procedures for sinus disease.

options.text: Balloon sinuplasty can be performed in office
options.rationale: Correct because the lecture lists balloon dilation as an option that can be done in the office or OR.

options.text: Open external sinus surgery only in office
options.rationale: Wrong because open external approaches are not described as office procedures in the lecture; it would be correct only if the slides specified external surgery as office-based.

options.text: Free flap reconstruction performed routinely in office
options.rationale: Wrong because free flap reconstruction is a complex head and neck oncology procedure done in the OR, not the office; it would be correct only if the lecture recommended office free flaps.

options.text: Cochlear implant surgery commonly done in office
options.rationale: Wrong because cochlear implantation is an operative procedure in the OR per the lecture; it would be correct only if the lecture stated otherwise.

options.text: Tonsillectomy routinely performed in office
options.rationale: Wrong because the lecture states tonsillectomy is an outpatient procedure but not an office procedure; it would be correct only if the slides recommended office tonsillectomy.

question: Which clinical red flag in the lecture suggests referral for head and neck oncology evaluation?
cited_learning_objective: Apply lecture guidance to identify concerning head and neck masses requiring oncology evaluation.

options.text: Persistent neck mass suspicious for malignancy
options.rationale: Correct because the lecture lists initial evaluations of head and neck masses and implies persistent masses warrant oncology assessment for SCC or other tumors.

options.text: Transient small lymph node after viral illness
options.rationale: Wrong because transient nodes after infection are less concerning; it would be correct only if persistence or other malignant features were present per the lecture.

options.text: Acute otitis media resolving with antibiotics
options.rationale: Wrong because resolving AOM is not a head and neck oncology red flag; it would be correct only if malignant features accompanied the ear disease.

options.text: Chronic sinus congestion without mass on exam
options.rationale: Wrong because chronic sinusitis alone without a mass is not the oncology referral trigger described in the lecture; it would be correct only if a suspicious mass were found.

options.text: Recurrent tonsillitis meeting frequency criteria
options.rationale: Wrong because recurrent tonsillitis is an indication for tonsillectomy, not necessarily oncology referral; it would be correct only if malignancy signs were present.

question: Which postoperative time window does the lecture highlight as a bleeding risk after tonsillectomy?
cited_learning_objective: Recall the timing of postoperative bleeding risk after tonsillectomy.

options.text: Twelve to fourteen days postoperatively risk
options.rationale: Correct because the lecture notes bleeding risk at 12-14 days after tonsillectomy.

options.text: First two hours only immediate risk
options.rationale: Wrong because while immediate bleeding can occur, the lecture specifically highlights the 12-14 day risk window; it would be correct only if the slides emphasized only immediate bleeding.

options.text: Six months postoperative chronic bleeding risk
options.rationale: Wrong because the lecture does not indicate a routine six-month bleeding risk; it would be correct only if late hemorrhage were documented in the slides.

options.text: No bleeding risk after discharge ever
options.rationale: Wrong because the lecture explicitly warns of bleeding risk post-discharge at 12-14 days; it would be correct only if the lecture denied any postoperative bleeding risk.

options.text: Bleeding risk only in first twenty four hours
options.rationale: Wrong because the lecture mentions both 24-hour and 12-14 day considerations, not exclusively the first 24 hours; it would be correct only if the later risk were absent.

question: Which ENT condition listed in the lecture is most directly treated with Botox or dermal fillers as part of its subspecialty practice?
cited_learning_objective: Associate facial plastics procedures with appropriate conditions.

options.text: Facial plastics uses Botox and dermal fillers
options.rationale: Correct because the lecture lists Botox and dermal fillers under Facial Plastics and Reconstruction.

options.text: Otology routinely uses Botox for hearing loss
options.rationale: Wrong because Otology focuses on hearing and balance, not cosmetic Botox; it would be correct only if Botox were indicated for an otologic condition in the lecture.

options.text: Laryngology uses dermal fillers for vocal cords
options.rationale: Wrong because dermal fillers are cosmetic facial procedures, not laryngeal interventions per the lecture; it would be correct only if fillers were used for vocal cord augmentation as described.

options.text: Rhinology uses Botox for sinus drainage
options.rationale: Wrong because the lecture does not list Botox for sinus drainage; it would be correct only if Botox were specified for rhinologic therapy.

options.text: Head and neck oncology uses fillers for reconstruction
options.rationale: Wrong because oncology reconstruction uses flaps and grafts rather than cosmetic dermal fillers per the lecture; it would be correct only if fillers were part of oncologic reconstruction.

question: Which statement from the lecture best summarizes the relationship between dizziness and ENT specialty involvement?
cited_learning_objective: Interpret when dizziness is within ENT scope versus other causes.

options.text: Dizziness is nonspecific and not always ENT
options.rationale: Correct because the lecture emphasizes that dizziness is non-specific and does not equal ENT pathology in all cases.

options.text: All dizziness should be managed by ENT only
options.rationale: Wrong because the lecture explicitly states dizziness is not synonymous with ENT disease and requires broader evaluation; it would be correct only if ENT managed all dizziness per the slides.

options.text: Vertigo always indicates central neurologic disease
options.rationale: Wrong because vertigo can be peripheral (e.g., BPPV) as described in the lecture; it would be correct only if central causes were the only etiology.

options.text: Lightheadedness is identical to vertigo clinically
options.rationale: Wrong because the lecture differentiates lightheadedness from vertigo; it would be correct only if the lecture equated the two.

options.text: Syncope is the same as benign positional vertigo
options.rationale: Wrong because syncope and BPPV are distinct entities per the lecture; it would be correct only if the lecture conflated syncope with vertigo.

question: A patient with chronic sinusitis and nasal polyps is considering immunotherapy. Which lecture-listed option addresses allergic contributors?
cited_learning_objective: Identify allergy-directed therapies mentioned for CRS management.

options.text: Sublingual immunotherapy or allergy shots
options.rationale: Correct because the lecture lists SLIT (sublingual immunotherapy) or allergy shots as management options for allergic contributors to CRS.

options.text: Systemic chemotherapy for sinonasal polyps
options.rationale: Wrong because chemotherapy is not listed for polyps in the lecture; it would be correct only if neoplastic disease required systemic therapy.

options.text: Topical otic drops for nasal polyps
options.rationale: Wrong because otic drops treat ear conditions, not nasal polyps; it would be correct only if topical nasal therapy were specified as otic drops.

options.text: Immediate endoscopic skull base resection
options.rationale: Wrong because skull base resection is for tumors, not routine allergic polyps per the lecture; it would be correct only if a tumor were present.

options.text: Routine systemic steroids indefinitely
options.rationale: Wrong because while steroids may be used, indefinite systemic steroids are not presented as standard in the lecture; it would be correct only if chronic systemic steroids were recommended.

question: Which ENT condition listed in the lecture is most commonly managed in pediatric practice with ear tubes?
cited_learning_objective: Recall common pediatric ENT procedures and indications.

options.text: Middle ear effusion treated with ear tubes
options.rationale: Correct because the lecture lists ear tubes for pediatric middle ear effusion and related hearing issues.

options.text: Adult chronic sinusitis treated with ear tubes
options.rationale: Wrong because ear tubes are a pediatric intervention for middle ear disease, not adult chronic sinusitis; it would be correct only if adult eustachian dysfunction required tubes per the lecture.

options.text: Vocal cord paralysis treated with ear tubes
options.rationale: Wrong because vocal cord paralysis is managed by Laryngology, not ear tubes; it would be correct only if ear tubes were indicated for concurrent middle ear disease.

options.text: Nasal polyps in children routinely treated with tubes
options.rationale: Wrong because nasal polyps are managed by Rhinology and not routinely with ear tubes; it would be correct only if eustachian dysfunction coexisted.

options.text: Thyroid nodules managed by ear tube placement
options.rationale: Wrong because thyroid nodules are unrelated to ear tube therapy; it would be correct only if the lecture linked thyroid disease to middle ear effusion.

question: Which surgical reconstruction technique mentioned in the lecture uses forehead tissue for nasal defects?
cited_learning_objective: Recognize the paramedian forehead flap as a reconstructive option.

options.text: Paramedian forehead flap for nasal reconstruction
options.rationale: Correct because the lecture references the paramedian forehead flap as a method to repair nasal defects and address cosmetic and obstruction concerns.

options.text: Skin graft from thigh for nasal lining
options.rationale: Wrong because while skin grafts are an option, the lecture specifically highlights the paramedian forehead flap for nasal reconstruction; it would be correct only if grafting were emphasized.

options.text: Free flap microvascular nasal reconstruction routinely
options.rationale: Wrong because free flaps are used in head and neck oncology reconstruction but the lecture specifically discusses the forehead flap for nasal defects; it would be correct only if free flaps were the chosen technique.

options.text: Local advancement flap from cheek for all defects
options.rationale: Wrong because local cheek flaps are not the specific paramedian forehead flap described; it would be correct only if cheek advancement were the recommended approach.

options.text: Observation without reconstruction for cosmetic issues
options.rationale: Wrong because the lecture lists reconstructive options including flaps and grafts rather than routine observation for significant nasal defects; it would be correct only if defects were minor.

question: Which condition listed in the lecture is caused by HPV types 6 and 11 and may require repeated airway procedures?
cited_learning_objective: Identify recurrent respiratory papillomatosis and its viral etiology.

options.text: Recurrent respiratory papillomatosis from HPV six eleven
options.rationale: Correct because the lecture names recurrent respiratory papillomatosis and attributes it to HPV types 6 and 11.

options.text: Laryngeal carcinoma caused by HPV sixteen eighteen
options.rationale: Wrong because the lecture specifically mentions HPV 6 and 11 for papillomatosis; it would be correct only if the slides linked carcinoma to HPV 16/18.

options.text: Chronic sinusitis due to HPV infection
options.rationale: Wrong because chronic sinusitis is not attributed to HPV in the lecture; it would be correct only if HPV were implicated in sinonasal disease per the slides.

options.text: Otitis media with effusion from HPV infection
options.rationale: Wrong because otitis media with effusion is not linked to HPV in the lecture; it would be correct only if HPV were documented as an otologic cause.

options.text: Thyroiditis caused by HPV infection
options.rationale: Wrong because thyroid disease is not associated with HPV in the lecture; it would be correct only if the lecture described such an association.

question: Which statement best reflects the lecture's description of general otolaryngology practice scope?
cited_learning_objective: Summarize the common conditions managed in general ENT practice.

options.text: General ENT manages hearing sinus voice pediatric oncology
options.rationale: Correct because the lecture lists hearing, sinus, voice, pediatric issues, and initial head and neck oncology evaluations as part of general otolaryngology.

options.text: General ENT exclusively performs cosmetic facial surgery
options.rationale: Wrong because while facial plastics is a subspecialty, general ENT covers a broader range including otology and rhinology; it would be correct only if practice were limited to cosmetics.

options.text: General ENT only treats pediatric ear disease
options.rationale: Wrong because general ENT treats adult and pediatric conditions across multiple domains per the lecture; it would be correct only if scope were restricted to pediatrics.

options.text: General ENT focuses solely on skull base tumors
options.rationale: Wrong because skull base tumors are a subspecialty area (otology/rhinology/skull base) rather than the sole focus of general ENT; it would be correct only if the practice were specialized.

options.text: General ENT never evaluates neck masses
options.rationale: Wrong because the lecture states general ENT includes initial evaluations of head and neck masses; it would be correct only if neck masses were excluded from ENT scope.

question: Which management option for otosclerosis is mentioned in the lecture as a treatment possibility?
cited_learning_objective: Recognize otosclerosis treatment options referenced in the lecture.

options.text: Otosclerosis treated with ossicular chain reconstruction
options.rationale: Correct because the lecture lists otosclerosis and OCR (ossicular reconstruction) as treatment options.

options.text: Otosclerosis managed only with intratympanic steroids
options.rationale: Wrong because intratympanic steroids are listed for SSNHL, not otosclerosis; it would be correct only if the lecture recommended steroids for otosclerosis.

options.text: Otosclerosis cured by nasal steroid sprays
options.rationale: Wrong because nasal steroids treat sinonasal disease, not otosclerosis; it would be correct only if otosclerosis were linked to nasal inflammation in the lecture.

options.text: Otosclerosis treated with tympanostomy tubes routinely
options.rationale: Wrong because tympanostomy tubes address middle ear effusion, not otosclerosis; it would be correct only if conductive loss were due to effusion.

options.text: Otosclerosis resolved by watchful waiting always
options.rationale: Wrong because the lecture lists surgical options like OCR for otosclerosis rather than always observing; it would be correct only if the slides recommended observation exclusively.

question: Which clinical feature in the lecture would most strongly suggest a central cause of vertigo rather than a peripheral one?
cited_learning_objective: Differentiate central versus peripheral vertigo features based on lecture content.

options.text: Focal neurological deficits accompany vertigo
options.rationale: Correct because the lecture indicates focal neurological findings point toward central causes rather than peripheral vestibular disorders.

options.text: Brief positional spinning triggered by head turn
options.rationale: Wrong because brief positional spinning is characteristic of peripheral BPPV per the lecture; it would be correct only if central positional vertigo were specified.

options.text: Three days of intense vertigo with nausea
options.rationale: Wrong because prolonged intense vertigo with nausea aligns with vestibular neuritis, a peripheral cause per the lecture; it would be correct only if central signs were present.

options.text: Fluctuating hearing loss with aural fullness
options.rationale: Wrong because fluctuating hearing loss and aural fullness suggest Meniere’s, a peripheral inner ear disorder; it would be correct only if central auditory pathways were implicated.

options.text: Anxiety without objective vestibular signs
options.rationale: Wrong because anxiety can mimic dizziness but lacks focal neuro signs that suggest central pathology; it would be correct only if neuro deficits were documented.

question: Which initial step does the lecture recommend when evaluating a patient with dizziness?
cited_learning_objective: List key components of the dizziness workup emphasized in the lecture.

options.text: Obtain a detailed history focusing on timing triggers
options.rationale: Correct because the lecture emphasizes history—when dizzy, triggers, duration, associated symptoms—as the cornerstone of dizziness evaluation.

options.text: Order immediate MRI for all dizzy patients
options.rationale: Wrong because the lecture stresses history and exam first rather than routine immediate MRI; it would be correct only if imaging were mandated for every case.

options.text: Prescribe vestibular suppressants before assessment
options.rationale: Wrong because the lecture prioritizes diagnostic history and exam before empiric therapy; it would be correct only if symptomatic suppression were necessary prior to evaluation.

options.text: Refer directly to psychiatry for anxiety evaluation
options.rationale: Wrong because while anxiety can cause dizziness, the lecture recommends medical history and physical exam to differentiate causes before psychiatric referral; it would be correct only if psychiatric causes were obvious.

options.text: Perform immediate surgical intervention for vertigo
options.rationale: Wrong because surgical intervention is not the initial step for dizziness per the lecture; it would be correct only if a surgical lesion were identified.

question: Which ENT condition from the lecture is explicitly associated with pediatric airway foreign bodies and tracheostomies?
cited_learning_objective: Recognize pediatric ENT issues including airway foreign bodies and tracheostomy indications.

options.text: Pediatrics handles airway foreign bodies and tracheostomies
options.rationale: Correct because the lecture lists pediatric ENT as managing airway foreign bodies and pediatric tracheostomies.

options.text: Rhinology manages pediatric tracheostomies routinely
options.rationale: Wrong because tracheostomies are managed in pediatric ENT rather than rhinology per the lecture; it would be correct only if rhinology were responsible for airway surgery.

options.text: Facial plastics performs pediatric airway foreign body removal
options.rationale: Wrong because facial plastics focuses on reconstruction and cosmetics, not airway foreign body removal in children; it would be correct only if reconstructive surgery were needed.

options.text: Otology treats pediatric tracheostomies commonly
options.rationale: Wrong because otology addresses ear and balance issues, not tracheostomies per the lecture; it would be correct only if otologic surgeons performed tracheostomies in that practice.

options.text: Head and neck oncology exclusively manages pediatric airway issues
options.rationale: Wrong because pediatric airway foreign bodies and tracheostomies are pediatric ENT concerns, not exclusively oncology; it would be correct only if oncologic airway disease predominated.

question: Which of the following best reflects the lecture's guidance about the role of history in dizziness evaluation?
cited_learning_objective: Recall the primacy of history-taking in dizziness assessment.

options.text: History is the most important part of workup
options.rationale: Correct because the lecture repeatedly emphasizes 'History, history, history' as central to dizziness evaluation.

options.text: Physical exam alone suffices without history
options.rationale: Wrong because the lecture stresses history first and not relying solely on physical exam; it would be correct only if history were unobtainable.

options.text: Imaging replaces history in most dizzy patients
options.rationale: Wrong because the lecture prioritizes history and exam over routine imaging; it would be correct only if imaging findings were necessary per the slides.

options.text: Dix-Hallpike is performed before any history
options.rationale: Wrong because the lecture suggests history guides the need for maneuvers like Dix-Hallpike; it would be correct only if positional vertigo were already certain.

options.text: Lab testing is the first diagnostic step always
options.rationale: Wrong because labs are not emphasized as the initial step in the lecture; it would be correct only if lab abnormalities were suspected.

question: Which ENT condition listed in the lecture commonly presents with nasal obstruction and may be structural or allergic in origin?
cited_learning_objective: Differentiate causes of nasal obstruction including structural and allergic etiologies.

options.text: Nasal obstruction from structural or allergic causes
options.rationale: Correct because the lecture states nasal obstruction can be due to structural problems, allergies, or chronic sinusitis.

options.text: Hearing loss primarily due to nasal obstruction
options.rationale: Wrong because hearing loss is a separate category; it would be correct only if eustachian dysfunction secondary to nasal obstruction caused middle ear issues per the lecture.

options.text: Vocal cord paralysis causing nasal obstruction
options.rationale: Wrong because vocal cord paralysis affects voice and airway, not nasal obstruction; it would be correct only if concurrent nasal pathology existed.

options.text: Tracheostomy as first-line for nasal obstruction
options.rationale: Wrong because tracheostomy addresses lower airway issues and is not a treatment for nasal obstruction per the lecture; it would be correct only if severe airway compromise extended to the lower airway.

options.text: Cholesteatoma presenting as nasal obstruction
options.rationale: Wrong because cholesteatoma is a middle ear pathology, not a cause of nasal obstruction; it would be correct only if sinonasal extension occurred per the lecture.

question: Which of the following best matches the lecture's description of management options for sudden sensorineural hearing loss?
cited_learning_objective: Apply lecture management options for SSNHL including steroid routes.

options.text: Oral steroids or intratympanic steroid therapy
options.rationale: Correct because the lecture lists oral steroids versus intratympanic (IT) steroids as treatment options for sudden sensorineural hearing loss.

options.text: Immediate tympanostomy tube placement universally
options.rationale: Wrong because tympanostomy tubes address middle ear effusion, not SSNHL, and are not listed as universal SSNHL therapy in the lecture; it would be correct only if conductive pathology were present.

options.text: High-dose systemic antibiotics as first-line
options.rationale: Wrong because antibiotics are not the primary therapy for SSNHL per the lecture; they would be correct only if an infectious middle ear cause were identified.

options.text: Observation without intervention for spontaneous recovery
options.rationale: Wrong because the lecture recommends steroid therapy rather than routine observation for SSNHL; it would be correct only if steroids were contraindicated.

options.text: Immediate cochlear implant placement emergently
options.rationale: Wrong because cochlear implantation is not an immediate emergency treatment for SSNHL per the lecture; it would be correct only if irreversible profound loss persisted after evaluation.

question: Which ENT subspecialty listed in the lecture commonly performs free flap reconstruction for large defects after tumor resection?
cited_learning_objective: Associate free flap reconstruction with Head and Neck Oncology practice.

options.text: Head and neck oncology performs free flap reconstruction
options.rationale: Correct because the lecture lists free flap reconstruction under Head & Neck Oncology for SCC and large defects.

options.text: Otology performs free flap reconstruction routinely
options.rationale: Wrong because otology focuses on ear and skull base procedures, not typically free flap reconstruction for head and neck tumors; it would be correct only if otology included major oncologic reconstruction in the lecture.

options.text: Rhinology exclusively uses free flaps for polyps
options.rationale: Wrong because rhinology treats polyps and sinus disease, not routine free flap reconstruction; it would be correct only if extensive skull base defects required flaps.

options.text: Facial plastics never involved in reconstruction
options.rationale: Wrong because facial plastics does perform reconstruction, but the lecture specifically attributes free flap reconstruction to Head & Neck Oncology; it would be correct only if facial plastics were excluded from reconstruction.

options.text: Pediatrics performs free flap reconstruction commonly
options.rationale: Wrong because pediatric ENT handles tonsils and tubes and pediatric airway issues, not routine free flap oncologic reconstruction; it would be correct only if pediatric tumors required such reconstruction per the lecture.

question: Which clinical scenario from the lecture would most likely prompt consideration of tympanoplasty?
cited_learning_objective: Apply lecture guidance to select candidates for tympanic membrane repair.

options.text: Persistent tympanic membrane perforation with hearing loss
options.rationale: Correct because the lecture lists tympanic membrane perforation with consideration of observation versus repair (tympanoplasty) as a management option.

options.text: Acute viral rhinitis with transient congestion only
options.rationale: Wrong because viral rhinitis does not indicate tympanoplasty; it would be correct only if a chronic perforation or conductive deficit were present.

options.text: Sudden sensorineural hearing loss without perforation
options.rationale: Wrong because SSNHL is treated with steroids, not tympanoplasty; it would be correct only if a perforation accompanied the hearing loss.

options.text: Nasal septal deviation causing obstruction
options.rationale: Wrong because septal deviation is a nasal structural issue managed by septoplasty, not tympanoplasty; it would be correct only if middle ear pathology existed.

options.text: Recurrent tonsillitis meeting frequency criteria
options.rationale: Wrong because recurrent tonsillitis indicates tonsillectomy, not tympanic membrane repair; it would be correct only if ear pathology coexisted.

question: Which lecture-listed feature distinguishes acute from chronic sinusitis by duration?
cited_learning_objective: Recall the temporal thresholds defining acute versus chronic sinusitis.

options.text: Acute under four weeks chronic over twelve weeks
options.rationale: Correct because the lecture defines acute sinusitis as less than four weeks and chronic as more than twelve weeks.

options.text: Acute over twelve weeks chronic under four weeks
options.rationale: Wrong because this reverses the lecture definitions; it would be correct only if the temporal thresholds were inverted in the slides.

options.text: Acute equals chronic regardless of duration
options.rationale: Wrong because the lecture differentiates acute and chronic by duration; it would be correct only if the lecture did not distinguish them.

options.text: Chronic defined as exactly eight weeks duration
options.rationale: Wrong because the lecture specifies greater than twelve weeks for chronicity, not eight weeks; it would be correct only if the slides used eight weeks as the cutoff.

options.text: Acute defined as more than twelve weeks duration
options.rationale: Wrong because acute is defined as under four weeks in the lecture; it would be correct only if the lecture mislabelled durations.
